title: Lesson 318
transcript: We've gone through an overview, an initial discussion of how we conduct investigations. Let's now delve a little more deeply into the digital forensics part of this. How do we conduct digital investigations? And One of the challenges we have with this is how do you actually apply all these investigative techniques to a digital environment where sometimes the evidence is a lot harder to find and extract and where reporting that evidence to people can often be difficult for them to understand and know truly what it means. So let's start with one of the first major challenges we have, and that's this concept of live evidence. So live evidence is data processes in a system that are running. So think of it this way. If you walk up to a computer system and you jiggle the mouse to wake that computer system up and you start checking to see what's in the browsing history and what files are stored, what are you doing to the state of that system? You're changing it. You are contaminating the evidence. And so one of the challenges we have is that when we conduct forensic investigations on computer systems, we want to try and capture all this live evidence, the CPU registers and cache, the data that's in RAM, the data that's in the network buffers, things like this. We want to try and capture as much of this data as we can while also corrupting that data as little as possible. And that's a very difficult thing to do. And so that's that concept you ever hear, if you hear of live evidence, it's capturing data from volatile memory. where even interacting with the system in any way corrupts that evidence and so this is a great example of where you need very specialized tools and knowledge to know how to do this in a proper way And this is really difficult on physical systems. It gets a lot easier if we talk about, say, virtual machines or systems in the cloud, because that's where we can use this idea of snapshotting the system or creating a complete copy mirror of the system, usually called a snapshot of a running system. We can create an exact copy of a running system without changing the state of that. the state of that system in the copy. Now, the major source of evidence you're going to look at when you're conducting forensic investigations on a computer system is the hard drive. And there's an important process that you need to go through when you're conducting investigations on the data on that hard drive. and that is you never want to touch the original drive. This is a perfect example. You take the original drive, you tag, bag, and carry it. You never do any analysis on that original drive because what might you do to that evidence? Corrupt it, contaminate it. So when you're dealing with hard drives, what you want to do is you want to take the original drive and make two copies. And then what you do is you want to make sure that they are exact copies. And I don't just mean like if you go into Windows, for instance, and you copy all the files out of the hard drive from a Windows system, what is actually being copied? Is it all the data on the hard drive? No. So in Windows, for instance, when you delete a file, what actually happens to that data? Is the data actually deleted? All that happens is Windows deletes the reference to the data. That data is still sitting there until later on when it might be overwritten. So when we talk about making copies of hard drives, what we're talking about doing is making a bit-for-bit identical copy of the original drive, which would capture any of that data that's sitting there, not referenced anymore, it's being deleted, but it's still sitting on the hard drive. And so when we talk about making two copies, they have to be exact bit for bit copies. And how would we verify that they prove that they're exact bit for bit copies? Well, you would hash the first drive, hash the two copies, and you would make sure that all three hash values are the same. And then you take the original drive, the first copy, put them in evidence bags, never touch them. And it's only that second copy that you actually perform your forensic investigations on. Now we briefly talked about this concept of the chain of custody in the previous video. And the whole idea behind the chain of custody, as we talked about, is to establish and associate it with the word control. Think of it this way. You show up in a court of law to present your evidence, to prove your case. And the judge or the defense attorney asks you, where was this evidence from this date to this date? And your answer is, You didn't have control over the evidence at that point. You have no idea where it was. Is that evidence going to be admissible? Is it going to be accepted? Of course not. So chain of custody means you've established control. You know exactly where that evidence was from the moment it was collected till years later where it might be used in a court of law for whatever type of investigation you're conducting. And you want to have control of that evidence over its entire life cycle. But evidence chain of custody by itself does not guarantee admissibility. And this is where we'll talk about the five rules of evidence, as they're often referred to. And the five rules of evidence are what are sort of required to achieve admissibility. So let's go through each of them, okay? Let's start with the first, which is authentic. The evidence has to be authentic. And what this ties back to is you are able to prove that the evidence actually came from the scene. And this goes back to where we talked about the first thing you do when you arrive at an investigation is you want to secure the scene. Take photographs, document where, what's where. And if you can say, look, here's the first photos I took when I arrived on site. There's the knife right there. O.J. Simpson's glove or whatever, you can prove the authenticity that the evidence actually came from the scene. The next is accurate. And accurate simply means the evidence has integrity, hasn't been tampered with. And like we just talked about with hard drives, you make bit for bit copies, hash the copies to make sure they are the same, the evidence has integrity. integrity. Next is complete. What this relates to is you're not allowed to, if you've collected some evidence that doesn't support your case, you're not allowed to just burn it or hide it or pull an Enron and shred it. Complete means all the evidence that you've collected you have to share with everyone and you're not allowed to hide any pieces that may not support your case. The next one is really challenging. The evidence must be convincing or reliable. There's actually another word I would associate with this, and that is that the evidence must demonstrate a high degree of veracity. What's veracity? Well, it's truth. The evidence must demonstrate a high degree of truth. And this is where we actually have quite a challenge in digital forensics, and that is that our evidence has to be convincing to someone. And if someone doesn't really understand computer systems and how they work, and what's this particular log event mean, and I'll hash the hard drive, and if they don't understand a lot of this very technical jargon, it's very difficult to make the evidence convincing to them to demonstrate to show that it has veracity and so this really this part here this rule of evidence really comes down to how you present the evidence and how you talk someone through what it means and what it's supposed to what it should actually be showing them And then the final piece is if you've achieved all these, hopefully the evidence will be admissible. That means it will be accepted, say in a court of law or by a regulator or whoever is conducting the investigation. Now let's talk through some of the techniques that you can use to conduct your investigation. One thing you could do is what's known as media analysis. Media analysis is where you're looking at the media, where the data is stored. So John would have talked through how you can do something known as purging, for instance, which means that the data, or sorry, let me use another example. John, for instance, talked through where you can do this concept of overwriting, where you overwrite the data with random ones and zeros. What do you recall about what he said about that? Is overwriting a defensible destruction technique? No, because it turns out people have studied this. If you just overwrite a hard drive, a hard drive is made up of the physical media of a hard drive. When you overwrite the data, there's still some data that remains from the original data and you can recover that. And so media analysis is this concept that you take the media, if it's a hard drive, a CD, a flash drive, an SSD drive, whatever it is, and you're doing very, very careful analysis of that media, typically to recover data that's being deleted or attempted to be destroyed. Next is software analysis. This is obviously where you're looking at the software. And what you're looking at here is you're trying to figure out exactly what did the software do? Was it trying to encrypt my data? Was it exfiltrating something? And you're also often trying to do what's known as attribution here. You're trying to figure out who wrote this software. And you can sometimes figure that out by looking at the names of files, the names of variables, things like that, because if someone speaks a certain language, maybe the variable names and stuff are in that certain language or certain types of hacker groups are known to use certain coding practices. So you might be able to figure that out. And then the final piece you could look at is network analysis. You're looking at how someone might have penetrated the network, how they might have traversed the network, what systems they might have infected. And in network analysis, your primary source of evidence is going to be log files. Now, the final sort of quick overview of digital forensics tools here is what are some of the techniques you can use? And I've mentioned a lot of these now. You can do malware analysis, log analysis, traces, those sorts of things. And what this all boils down to is you need to be able to report and document your findings. And the important part to understand here is something we've talked about a few times now, and that is you've got to think very carefully about who is your audience. If you're reporting to senior management, if you're reporting to the media, if you're reporting to a judge or a jury, the way that you format, the way that you present, the level of detail is going to vary by audience. So the final piece you have to think about when you're conducting your investigation is, who's the audience? What way do I present this evidence to them so that it's gonna have veracity, so they understand it?
